sections.paragraph:
εὐστάθεια, ας, ἡ (s. two next entries; Epicurus in Plut., Mor., 135c; Epict. 2, 5, 9; Vett. Val. 183, 3; SIG 1109, 15; OGI 669, 4 and 45; PGiss 87, 18; LXX; EpArist 216 and 261; Philo; Jos., Ant. 18, 207) a favorite term for describing stable political conditions, then state or condition of being free from internal agitation, good disposition, tranquility, stability, firmness 1 Cl 61:1 (w. εἰρήνη as Philo, De Jos. 57, In Flacc. 135); 65:1.—DELG s.v. στάθμη.

Epicurus Epicurus , IV–III b.c.—List 5

Plut Plut , I–II a.d.—List 5

Epict Epict , various works, I–II a.d.—List 5

SIG SIG = Sylloge Inscriptionum Graecarum3; superscript omitted in text—List 3

OGI OGI = Orientis Graeci Inscriptiones Selectae—List 3

PGiss PGiss = Griechische Papyri . . . zu Giessen—List 4

LXX LXX = Septuaginta, ed. ARahlfs, unless otherwise specified—Lists 2, beg.

EpArist EpArist = Epistle of Aristeas, II b.c.—List 5

Philo Philo = P. of Alexandria, I b.c.–I a.d.—List 5

Jos. Jos. = Josephus. This abbr. used when follow by title; I a.d.—Lists 5

1 Cl 1 Cl = 1 Clement—List 1

DELG DELG = PChantraine, Dictionnaire étymologique de la langue grecque—List 6

s.v. s.v. = sub voce (under the word, look up the word)

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 413
